description: The entity enforces authentication controls through configured identity and sign-in settings, including MFA or equivalent strong authentication, password and session controls, and centralized identity or SSO configuration where applicable.

subcriteria.name: authentication_enforcement
subcriteria.reference_text: An admin settings page, configuration export, or security policy shows authentication controls are enforced rather than merely recommended. Real evidence usually says MFA required, multi-factor authentication enforced, sign-in policy applied, conditional access enabled, legacy authentication disabled, or a rule scoped to all users, admins, or a defined group.

subcriteria.name: password_and_session_settings
subcriteria.reference_text: A password or sign-in policy shows concrete configuration values such as minimum password length, complexity or banned password rules, failed login lockout, and session or idle timeout. Real evidence often appears as a table or settings screen with values like 12 characters, 5 failed attempts, or 15 minutes idle timeout.

subcriteria.name: identity_provider_or_sso_configuration
subcriteria.reference_text: An identity platform or app configuration shows centralized authentication through an identity provider or single sign-on. Real evidence usually mentions Okta, Auth0, Entra ID, Google Workspace, identity provider, SSO, SAML, OAuth, OIDC, assigned applications, or sign-in methods marked active or enabled.

mismatch_signals.pattern: \bplease enable mfa\b|\benable mfa by\b|\bmfa reminder\b|\brollout email\b|\bemail asking employees to enable mfa\b
mismatch_signals.reason: A reminder or rollout email is not proof that authentication controls are enforced.
User lifecycle evidence is related to access administration, not direct proof of authentication settings.
A basic login page does not by itself prove MFA, password policy, or SSO enforcement.
